name: Negation: ne ... pas
summary: French wraps the verb in two words, ne and pas, to make it negative.
explanation:
French negation has two parts that hug the verb: ne goes before it and pas goes after. Je parle (I speak) becomes je ne parle pas (I do not speak).

Before a vowel, ne shortens to n': je n'aime pas (I don't like). In casual spoken French the ne is often dropped, leaving just pas, but in writing and careful speech you should keep both.

Other negatives replace pas: ne ... jamais (never), ne ... rien (nothing), ne ... plus (no longer), ne ... personne (nobody). The ne stays; the second word changes.
pattern: ne + verb + pas  (ne becomes n' before a vowel)

examples.en: He doesn't like coffee. (n' before a vowel)

examples.native: Elle ne mange jamais de viande.

commonMistakes: Using only one part in writing: it is ne ... pas, not just ne or just pas.
Forgetting to elide: it is je n'aime pas, not je ne aime pas.
Adding pas to other negatives: it is ne ... jamais, not ne ... jamais pas.
Translating English 'do not' literally. French has no 'do'; ne and pas wrap the verb.
Placing pas before the verb. It always comes after the conjugated verb.
tip: Picture the verb in a sandwich: ne on one side, pas on the other. Swap pas for jamais, rien, or plus to change the meaning.
